exemplar.title: An Investigation into the Impact of Leading Questions on the Theory of Reconstructive

detailedExemplarData.title: An Investigation into the Impact of Leading Questions on the Theory of Reconstructive

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim and real-world relevance are clearly stated and expertly explained
Theory is accurately described and explicitly linked to the investigation design
Both null and directional hypotheses fully operationalize IV and DV, including units
weaknesses: Slight misquoting of Loftus & Palmer question wording
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets all topâband descriptors: the aim is not only stated but its relevance to legal eyewitness testimony is thoroughly explained; the reconstructive-memory theory is accurately described and explicitly connected to the verb manipulation; and both hypotheses fully operationalize IV and DV with units. The minor stylistic and quotation issues do not detract from criterion fulfillment.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is not only described but explained in terms of avoiding order effects and demand characteristics
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are described and justified with ethical and practical rationale
Controlled variables and material choices are clearly explained in relation to internal validity
weaknesses: Inconsistent appendix labeling (e.g., Appendix F cited twice)
Ethical justification for deception could be expanded
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three exploration strands satisfy the highest descriptor in the 3â4 band: design, sampling, and control of variables are not merely described but explained with clear rationale. Minor presentation and ethical justification suggestions do not undermine the full achievement of this criterion.

strengths: Choice of MannâWhitney U is appropriate and well justified with reference to data distribution
Descriptive statistics (means, SDs) are calculated and reported
Inferential test is applied correctly in principle
weaknesses: Numerical inconsistencies between text means and bar-chart values
Graph axes are mislabelled, producing misleading heights
Conflicting U-values reported between main text and appendix
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis applies both descriptive and inferential statistics appropriately but contains multiple calculation and presentation errors, placing it securely in the 3â4 bandâs upper level (max 4). The rationale for non-parametric testing is strong, but the numerical and graphical inaccuracies prevent a higher mark.

strengths: Findings are discussed with explicit reference to reconstructive-memory theory
Strengths and limitations are thoroughly explained and directly linked to validity or reliability
Modifications are explicitly tied to stated limitations and fully justified
weaknesses: Discussion could be deepened by exploring the mechanism of schema activation in the reversed pattern
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All evaluation strands meet the 5â6 band descriptors: findings are discussed (not just described) in relation to theory, strengths and limitations are both explained and relevant, and modifications directly address limitations with full justification. Minor suggestions do not detract from the exemplary overall performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart is provided and relates to the hypothesis, but axis labelling/values (bar tops ~140 km/h despite text means â62 km/h) indicate substantial inaccuracy.  Thus the graph addresses the hypothesis but contains clear errors, placing it in the middle of the 3-4 band; a 3 is appropriate.

Findings are stated, significance is noted, and the student links results to the hypothesis, yet the interpretation is confused (reversed means vs expectation) and partly contradictory.  Because interpretation is present but flawed, the work edges into the 3-4 band but not its top; a 3 best reflects this mixed quality.

Both null and directional research hypotheses are presented.  The IV is fully operationalised as the specific verb used in the critical question; the DV is operationalised as a numerical speed estimate in km/h.  Units are explicitly stated.  This meets all descriptors for the 5-6 band with no discernible omission, so the top of the band (6) is justified.

Opportunity sampling is named; inclusion/exclusion (age, language, psychology students) and rationale (ease, ethics, demand characteristics) are given, together with demographic details and random assignment to conditions.  These constitute an explanation rather than mere description, satisfying the top of the 3-4 band.

Reconstructive-memory theory is outlined with Bartlett and schema references, and the narrative shows how leading questions should alter recall via schema-driven reconstruction.  The link between the theory and the planned manipulation (verbs) is explicitly spelled out.  Description is complete and accurate, with only minor wording flaws, meriting the upper band; a 5 reflects a secure, though not exemplary, execution.

The student relates results to reconstructive-memory theory and discusses why the pattern differs from Loftus & Palmer, but the discussion is brief and lacks depth on theoretical implications.  This fits the descriptor âfindings are described with reference to background theory,â meriting a mid-band 3 within 3-4.

The student clearly states the aim: to investigate whether verbs of different emotional intensity (âsmashedâ vs âcollidedâ) influence speed estimates, and explicitly relates this to reconstructive memory and the legal importance of eyewitness testimony.  The real-world relevance (accuracy of court evidence) is explicitly explained rather than merely identified.  Minor stylistic awkwardness does not detract from a full, accurate explanation, so a solid performance in the 5-6 band is warranted; a mid-band 5 is most appropriate.

Multiple strengths (diverse opportunity sample, control of extraneous variables, independent-measures advantages) and limitations (non-drivers, unit ambiguity, individual differences) are each explained and directly linked to validity or reliability.  The depth of explanation justifies placement in the 5-6 band; a 5 is assigned to reflect solid but not exceptional detail.

The student lists several controlled variables (standardised instructions, identical video, environment, questionnaire order) and justifies each in terms of internal validity and extraneous-variable control.  This moves beyond description into explanation, fully meeting the 3-4 bandâs upper level.

Each limitation is accompanied by a concrete, justified modification (recruit experienced drivers, specify units, use matched-pairs design) clearly linked to how it addresses the stated weakness.  This meets the highest descriptor requirements; a 5 (rather than 6) acknowledges strong but concise justification.

The independent-measures design is not only described but also explained in terms of avoiding order effects and demand characteristics, and random allocation procedures are detailed.  This explanatory depth fits the 3-4 band; the clarity and appropriateness support the maximum score of 4 within that band.

Means, SDs and a Mann-Whitney U test are used; the rationale for a non-parametric test is correctly given.  However, numerical inconsistencies are evident (bar-chart means mis-match text; some rank sums deviate from sample size), indicating minor errors.  This matches the 3-4 descriptor of appropriate statistics "but contain errors"; a 4 reflects solid yet imperfect work.

annotations.comment: The background discussion accurately summarises Bartlettâs reconstructive memory theory but could strengthen the link to your specific manipulation (verbs) by explicitly stating how schema activation will differ for âsmashedâ versus âcollidedâ.

The description of Loftus and Palmerâs (1974) study is clear, but the critical question wording is slightly misquoted. Ensure consistency in phrasing (e.g., âsmashed/hit/bumpedâ) to avoid misrepresenting the original research.
The aim is concisely stated and its relevance to legal eyewitness testimony is well explained, demonstrating a strong introduction that meets top-band criteria.
The operationalization of the independent variable (verb emotional intensity) is precise, naming both conditions. This clarity supports reproducibility.
The null hypothesis is stated clearly, but you may strengthen it by reasserting the exact operational definitions of both IV and DV to reinforce experimental clarity.
The directional hypothesis is well formulated, linking higher emotional intensity (âsmashedâ) to increased speed estimates, and aligns with theory-based expectations.
The justification for nonâparametric MannâWhitney U is appropriate and explicitly linked to data characteristicsâthis reasoning strengthens analysis.
The bar chart axes are mislabelled: bar heights (~140 km/h) do not match reported means (~62 km/h). Replot with correct scale and labels.
The interpretation suggests the data contradicts the hypothesis but could more directly link statistical values (means, SDs) to theoretical expectation.
The independent-measures design is clearly explained with rationale for random allocation and avoidance of demand characteristicsâan excellent justification.
The sampling technique is justified well: opportunity sampling with exclusions based on age and psychology background shows ethical and demand-characteristic considerations.
The materials and procedure list is comprehensive but check appendix references (Appendix F cited twice for different elements); maintain consistent labeling to avoid confusion.
The rationale for controlling instructions, environment, and stimuli is detailed and clearly linked to internal validityâwell articulated.
The ethical considerations cover consent, withdrawal, anonymity, and debriefing, but the justification for deception and its ethical approval process could be expanded.
The choice to report means and standard deviations for ordinal data requires justification; consider reporting medians and interquartile ranges for nonâparametric context.
The rationale for using the MannâWhitney U test is well justified in relation to design and distribution; commendable analytic reasoning.
The reported U-value (108) conflicts with Appendix Iâs U=292 for sample 2. Reconcile these inconsistencies and ensure correct reporting of inferential results.
The evaluation references reconstructive memory theory but remains surface-level. Deepen discussion by exploring why schema activation might reverse expected patterns.
Linking sample diversity to population validity is sound, but consider the limitations of opportunity sampling when inferring to broader legal populations.
Recruiting experienced drivers is a logical modification to address schema familiarity limitations; consider specifying driver licensure criteria.
Noting unit ambiguity in speed estimates is insightful. The proposed unit-specification modification directly addresses data integrity.
Suggesting a matched-pairs design to control individual differences is well justified; consider detailing matching variables (e.g., gaming experience, driving exposure).
The conclusion reports p<0.005 but earlier text states p=0.00657. Ensure consistency in p-value reporting to maintain credibility.
References lack consistent formatting (APA or Harvard). Include year in parentheses, italics for journal titles, and DOIs where available.
Outlier speeds (e.g., 175.0 km/h) are flagged but no sensitivity analysis is reported. Consider re-running descriptive and inferential tests with outliers removed to assess their impact.
The calculation table uses \mu_A for both conditions, which may confuse readers. Label means distinctly (e.g., \mu_Collided, \mu_Smashed) for clarity.
Appendix I presents U-values for both samples but the combined significance section is vague. Include exact p-value calculation steps or test output for transparency.
